industry.headline: SECI Issues Transformer Tender for 700 MW Solar Project
industry.summary: The Solar Energy Corporation of India (SECI) has invited bids for engineering, design, manufacturing, testing, and supply of five 160 MVA, 220/33-33 KV power transformers for a 700 MW solar project at Radhanesda in Gujarat. The tender includes specifications for technical parameters and compliance. Successful bidders will deliver transformers per agreed terms. This procurement aims to expand solar infrastructure in India.

This initiative aims to address growing EV adoption and infrastructure gaps in the state.

tech.headline: Solar cell efficiency tables updated to Version 68
tech.summary: Professor Martin Green's research group published Version 68 of solar cell efficiency tables, featuring 21 new results. The update reflects advancements in photovoltaic technology, providing a comprehensive overview of current efficiency benchmarks for solar cells globally.

tech.headline: Chinese researchers achieve 33.33% efficiency in tandem solar cells
tech.summary: CAS-led researchers developed a peak-selective passivation strategy using aluminum oxide nanocoating to address leakage in pyramid-textured silicon for perovskite-silicon tandem cells. The method is compatible with existing silicon manufacturing and enables high-efficiency, stable devices reaching around 33% efficiency.

learn.title: Smart Grids and Advanced Metering
learn.intro: Modern power networks must balance variable renewable output, demand spikes, and resilience. Smart grids equipped with advanced metering transform electricity from a one‑way flow into a responsive, data‑driven system, essential for decarbonising the grid and empowering consumers.
learn.c1_text: Think of the grid as a two‑lane highway: traditional grids are one‑way, while smart grids allow cars (energy) to travel both ways. Sensors and communication let utilities see real‑time load, enabling demand‑response actions that can shave up to 10% of peak demand.
learn.c2_text: Smart meters record electricity use in 15‑minute intervals, sending data via secure networks. This granularity lets utilities forecast demand more accurately and consumers monitor usage, often cutting bills by 5‑15% through informed behavior.
learn.c3_text: India’s Smart Grid Mission targets 35 GW of smart meters by 2025, aiming to reduce technical losses from 22% to below 15%. Pilot projects in Delhi and Karnataka demonstrate AI‑driven voltage control, improving reliability for millions.
learn.stat1: Global smart meter deployments surpassed 1.2 billion units in 2023
learn.stat2: IEA projects 70% of electricity customers will have smart meters by 2030
learn.stat3: India installed 30 million smart meters in 2022, a 40% increase YoY
learn.stat4: Smart grid investments expected to reach $300 billion annually by 2030

learn.title: Wave Energy Converters Overview
learn.intro: Ocean waves store immense kinetic energy—enough to power the world many times over. Converting this motion into electricity offers a predictable, low‑carbon source that complements wind and solar, especially for coastal nations seeking energy independence.
learn.c1_text: Imagine a buoy bobbing on waves like a child on a swing. The up‑and‑down movement drives hydraulic pistons or linear generators, turning wave height (2‑3 m typical) into up to 500 kW per device.
learn.c2_text: Key designs include point absorbers (small floating devices), oscillating water columns (air chambers that pulse with wave pressure), and attenuators (long hinged segments). Each captures energy differently but shares common control electronics.
learn.c3_text: India’s Ministry of Power funded a 150 kW oscillating water column in Gujarat’s Kutch region, aiming to scale to 10 MW by 2027, supporting coastal electrification and desalination.
learn.stat1: World wave energy potential estimated at 3 TW, 10× current global electricity demand
learn.stat2: US DOE reports 25 operational wave farms worldwide as of 2024
learn.stat3: India’s coastal length exceeds 7,500 km, offering vast wave resource
learn.stat4: Average wave power density off India's west coast: 30‑40 kW/m

learn.title: Direct Air Carbon Capture with Renewables
learn.intro: Removing CO₂ straight from the atmosphere is a vital tool for net‑zero goals. When powered by renewable electricity, Direct Air Capture (DAC) becomes a climate‑negative technology, turning captured carbon into fuels or permanent storage.
learn.c1_text: DAC units use large fans to move 1,000 m³ of air per minute over chemical sorbents that bind CO₂. Each megawatt of renewable power can capture roughly 0.5 tonnes of CO₂ daily, comparable to the emissions of 100 cars.
learn.c2_text: Coupling DAC with solar or wind avoids fossil‑fuel electricity. Storage batteries smooth intermittent supply, ensuring the sorbent regeneration cycle runs continuously, boosting capture efficiency to over 90%.
learn.c3_text: India’s National Hydrogen Mission includes a pilot DAC plant in Gujarat, slated to capture 1 MtCO₂ per year using 300 MW of solar power, targeting export of green synthetic fuels.
learn.stat1: Global DAC capacity reached 0.2 MtCO₂/year in 2023
learn.stat2: IEA estimates DAC could remove 10‑15 GtCO₂ annually by 2050
learn.stat3: Cost of renewable‑powered DAC fell to $120 per tonne CO₂ in 2024
learn.stat4: India aims to sequester 5 MtCO₂ annually via DAC by 2035

learn.title: Small Wind Turbines for Homes
learn.intro: Distributed wind turbines let households and villages generate clean power on‑site, reducing grid dependence and providing resilience during outages. Small‑scale turbines complement rooftop solar, especially in windy rural areas.
learn.c1_text: A turbine with a 3‑m rotor sweeps an area of about 7 m². At 6 m/s wind speed, it can produce 1 kW, enough to run a typical refrigerator and lighting for a small home.
learn.c2_text: The generator’s AC output is converted by an inverter to match household voltage. Modern turbines include MPPT controllers that maximize output across variable wind speeds, similar to solar optimizers.
learn.c3_text: The Ministry of New & Renewable Energy installed 15 kW turbines in 200 villages of Rajasthan, delivering 30 % of local electricity demand and reducing diesel generator use by 400 kWh per month.
learn.stat1: Worldwide installed capacity of small wind (<100 kW) reached 2 GW in 2023
learn.stat2: Average capacity factor for community turbines: 25‑30 %
learn.stat3: India’s small wind market grew 12 % YoY in 2023
learn.stat4: A 5 kW turbine can offset ~7 tCO₂ annually, equivalent to planting 200 trees

learn.title: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission
learn.intro: Hydrogen produced with renewable electricity—green hydrogen—offers a zero‑emission fuel for industry, transport, and power. India’s mission sets ambitious targets and incentives to jump‑start a domestic hydrogen economy and cut fossil fuel imports.
learn.c1_text: The plan aims for 5 GW of electrolyzer capacity by 2030, producing roughly 200 kt of green hydrogen per year—enough to fuel 1 million fuel‑cell vehicles or replace 10 Mt of coal in steelmaking.
learn.c2_text: Projects receive a 3.5 % capital subsidy, tax exemptions on equipment, and a guaranteed offtake price of $2.5/kg for the first five years, making green hydrogen competitive with grey hydrogen at $3‑4/kg.
learn.c3_text: Gujarat launched a 100 MW solar‑powered electrolyzer in 2024, expected to generate 4 kt of hydrogen annually for local fertilizer plants, showcasing the mission’s regional rollout.
learn.stat1: India currently imports ~75 Mt of hydrogen‑equivalent fossil fuels per year
learn.stat2: Target green hydrogen production: 10 Mt by 2040
learn.stat3: Renewable electricity capacity in India: 230 GW (2024)
learn.stat4: Projected reduction in CO₂ emissions from hydrogen: 120 Mt by 2035

learn.title: How Wind Turbines Generate Electricity
learn.intro: Wind turbines turn the kinetic energy of moving air into electrical power, forming a cornerstone of the clean‑energy mix. Understanding their aerodynamics and generators helps demystify the technology behind the iconic turbine silhouettes.
learn.c1_text: Blade profiles act like airplane wings, creating lift as wind passes. A 50‑m rotor captures wind over 2,000 m²; at 8 m/s, it can generate 2 MW—enough for 500 homes.
learn.c2_text: Lift spins a low‑speed shaft, which drives a gearbox (or direct‑drive system) to increase rotation to 1,500 rpm, turning a generator that produces AC electricity synchronized to the grid frequency.
learn.c3_text: India’s wind farms in Tamil Nadu and Gujarat collectively added 7 GW in 2023, with turbine hubs reaching 120 m height, boosting capacity factors to 35 % in high‑wind corridors.
learn.stat1: Global wind capacity reached 1,200 GW in 2023
learn.stat2: Average capacity factor for onshore wind: 30‑40 %
learn.stat3: India installed 10.5 GW of wind capacity in 2023, 12 % of world total
learn.stat4: Each 2 MW turbine offsets ~3,500 tCO₂ annually
